sentences.text: I try to-- I try to remember it.
Those days, everything was upside down.
The trains were-- people were going on-- outside and inside the trains.
And by the time we got home, everybody was telling me that at least you are going to find somebody.
And it was the same day, I remember.
My brother-- my only brother, my older brother than-- he's older, a few years, he was home.
He was liberated in Budapest.
Naftali, or Joseph.
He was in Budapest.
He was working in a bakery, and he helped a lot of his-- a lot of his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] persecuted brothers with bread.
He was pretty handy and lucky, thank God.
He didn't go through any of this, what you call, physical experiences what I went through.
And he was liberated in Budapest.
He lived with the-- I think with the-- I think Gentile papers he had.
But he was lucky, too.
He was in a good position, and he was home already.
My father was home, but he-- I wasn't sure about that.
In--in Budapest, I did hear that my father survived too, but I wasn't sure where to find him.
And I came from the train with Krakowich, Bela, this poor friend of mine who lost his brother over there in-- in Loretto.
And we came to the congregational building.
And they-- right away, somebody ran over to call my father.
And I-- it was some reunion.
And the same afternoon, my brother saw me, and he got the news from girls, probably the same day or a day later, that I'm the only one who did survive it.
And others-- we were a family of eight, and now we are three.
So both of these shocks came simultaneously-- my arrival home and the same news.
But he did not to tell my father.
He did not to have my father should know it.
He knew it, but he didn't want my father should know about it that he met some girls who were in Bergen-Belsen, and they told him, yes, that Greenfeld Nini or Mrs.
Greenfeld.
Yes, she passed away on the same day when the English army marched in.
And my brother was-- he just couldn't express his big joy by meeting me, because deep down he knew that there is-- there is plenty sad story behind it.
And so he stayed in Tornal'a.
And few days later, I think it was around Tisha B'Av-- which was summertime-- I did find out definitely that my mother and that my sisters did die.
And I was sitting shiva for one hour.
I don't-- I didn't even question the rabbinical authorities about it, because I know if you hear a-- a-- within 30 days, if you hear it, you have to sit shiva.
If it is more than 30 days, you only sit one hour-- that's the law.
So I remember Tisha B'Av, it was anyway a sad day in our-- in our calendar.
And I was sitting alone.
I didn't want my father to see me.
And I was sitting shiva that time for the death of my mother.
Well, what should I tell you?
The town was they-- they tried to figure out who did survive, who did not survive.
And the fact-- the bitter fact was that they had about 700 people before the war, 140-- 160 families, was totally about 700 people.
And now they survive about 125.
This was the ratio, in general, and some places, it was even worse.
In Poland, I don't even want to talk about it, out of 3 and 1/2 million, we came back about 40,000.
In Tornal'a, we stayed-- when my father tried to establish again his business contacts, but our root, for the business, was in Lucenec, which is about 60 kilometers more to the west from Tornal'a.
It's about an hour with the train, not more than that, an hour.
That's where my mother's parents, and my mother's family had a huge, big business before the war.
Tornal'a was just a branch of that.
Lucenec, where my mother was from, is a much bigger town.
Lucenec today has also about 30,000 people, hardly any Jews.
But they still have a temple, which is the most magnificent building I ever saw in a temple.
With the rich modern city, and today there are no Jews, but because of its beauty, architectural beauty, the government keeps the-- keeps the temple, just for a show, whatever it is.
So my father and the family did see that in Tornal'a, to establish something, would be very, very hard.
Unfortunately, we didn't have anybody to take care of the household, keep the household.

I forgot to mention that in every town, they established the congregation, the community, what they call a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kitchen, I think it was supported by the Joint, for the survivor-- for the surviving, we call it [NON-ENGLISH]-- they called it.
Repatriated, or returned, survivors of the Holocaust had a kitchen, which was supplied or supported by the, I think, by the Joint.
Every town had that.
So it happened that I was, yes, in Tornal'a for a few weeks, a few months maybe, a few months.

Must have been about four or five months, or six months, I remember that I even participated in a project because the community wanted to make a memorial book with the names of those people who did not survive.
And every-- and the Dr.
Reichman, who was the president of the community, they knew that I know how to do art work and-- and writing.
So I was doing that job.
In the meantime, my father tried to establish contacts from Lucenec to establish the old big business.
There were buildings and huge yards-- huge yards of porcelain.
And my grandfather's family had the big porcelain wholesale business-- besides the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] junkyard of metal and textile, and all these things.
So my father try to establish and pick up the pieces.
In Lucenec we found out, also, that from my grandfather's-- my grandmother's 45 grandchildren, six of us did survive.
Six or seven.
Seven of us did survive.
In other words, my mother's sister had three sons, and my mother's other brother had also two sons who did survive.
None of the 10 sisters and brothers of my mother survived.
None of them.
And they were not old people.
The oldest was probably about 50 to 55.
Yes, I'd say it's a hobby of mine.
I don't know whether it's a talent.
I do remember the connections.
I know some strange people come to me, whether I know who is related to whom and how.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Yes.
Yes, he went to Prague.
He went to-- actually, he wanted to be busy.
Instead of sitting and mourning, he wanted to pick up the pieces.
And we went through a very hard-- a very hard time, because my father was very emotional.
And I noticed, I saw, and I heard his nightly cries.
My mother and my father, they were cousins besides that, and for him it was like the end of the world.
And he wanted to be busy.
And it meant-- he went-- some people came over there, but Mr.
Greenfeld, you just cannot sit back and mourn.
Life has to go on.
Somebody made even a mention, made the remark, my own teacher said, that some people give up, and don't intend to establish a new family, get married.
And he says, I-- I don't want to have anything after this.
Everything is lost.
People were very much, very much depressed.
So these men, he said, this is the same thing like if you would put oil on fire.
You want to put out the fire with oil, with benzine, or oil.
That way, not the other way around.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH], they say it in Yiddish.
Just because this happened, we have to-- we have to re-establish.
We have to reinforce, and-- and establishing and building up new families.
And that the future is not lost, despite of all this.
You had to be great in courage, and in mind, to come up with such-- with such material to do, to try to convince people.
But the facts did show-- look what happens here-- There's a book, what out of the ashes, we came out.

I don't know whether you read about it, but I read about it that 80% of the French soldiers-- this is nothing with me personally-- 80% of them, they came home who were-- who were in German captivity, POWs, 80% of them, mentally cockeyed.
And probably it has to do with our fate, that not everything is lost.
It's easier to say than to-- than to live up to it.
But it's-- the other way around, it would be even worse.
As a matter of fact, because of this-- and I saw some of the people who were trying to-- and many people lost their faiths-- no question about it.
I went back to-- I went-- I went to yeshiva, because I think I needed more encouragement and strength in myself that-- not to give up.
The yeshiva is in the eastern-- in the western part of Slovakia.
It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
As a matter of fact, the picture I have-- I have here a picture here of the whole group, of about 40 boys, that we came out.
The American army came, said they'd visit us, many of their rabbis from the US came to visit us.
And I was one of those who was-- who became a part of this, and came out with them.
We got student-- student visas at the end of 1946, came out to America.
It was also not the easy thing, because I have seen my father-- my father's tears.
He didn't want to part from me.
He said, that's all I have.
My brother was a little bit older, and he got married.
And this is the-- I would say, the story while I was-- while I was still in Czechoslovakia.
A genius, a giant, a brain which is one in 500 years is born, a man with his qualities.
He lived 54 years.
Yesterday we were out at his grave.
Anyway, the yeshiva came out here, and we didn't come straight.
As a matter of fact, I have to tell you this little story, that the yeshiva was already in Paris.
And me, I was still struggling with myself, did not leave my father alone.
I heard his-- and heard and saw his tears at nights, that if I'm going to leave, he's going to be totally, totally alone.
And I just-- I just said, I'm going to-- I'm not going to leave him alone.
I'm not going to leave him here should stay alone.
All of a sudden he got a letter from my-- from the dean of the yeshiva.
He used the terminology from the Bible, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when Joseph's brethren wanted to sell him, so they-- and the oldest one-- he says, [SPEAKING HEBREW], don't you send me this child.
Don't you send me this son.
So this dean of my--from the yeshiva wrote to my father, don't you send me this son of yours.
If you're going to stay there, the country's going communistic.
There's no future-- there's no future for him to stay there.
Don't you keep him back.
The fact is that my father did not keep me back.
It was just my feelings toward him that I didn't want to hurt him by my leaving.
Finally, he approved it, and he came out.
I came out about three weeks after everybody was already on the road with the ship.
That was a few years later.
Well, I brought him out as an American citizen.
My father got married-- married my beautiful, sweet stepmother, the lady of one in a million.
There's a picture of her here.
I've not spoke to you about that.
And they lived a beautiful, I would say-- I don't know exactly, but over 25 years.
It was a most beautiful union.
They were first cousins, and it was beautiful, so far beautiful that this lady is still our guest, when she just has the strength to come here.
Here-- years ago, every Shabbos he was here, every Yom Tov he was here.
Now, a little bit less because she's not that strong.
This was the story of my coming out.
I brought her-- I brought him out, then I brought her out, because she was actually not my mother.

I established a rule-- I found a-- I found a loophole which says-- I found it in the application for the immigration office-- that if your stepparent becomes your stepparent before your-- before your 18th birthday, she has the same rights as a real parent.
I came up to the 70 Columbus, and I showed them this paragraph-- in the small letters, small prints-- I said, have you never heard about this.
I said, I didn't put it in.
Look at it.
And they went up to the supervisor, after supervisor's supervisor.
And he says, this young man is right.
So it was-- it was one of those things I'm very proud of that I did find a legal way to have her out also.
Today, the-- the-- the doors are open for every-- they don't ask questions.
Today you don't need-- those days when we came here, you had to have all the papers, plus a financial certificate.
Somebody had to guarantee that you're not going to fall on the government's neck.
They're not going to-- you're going to be on your own.
They wouldn't have to support you.
But those days, it was hard.
Today those-- the gates are open.
Every schlimazel could come in, and they do.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This has nothing to do with me, personally, this is just a general statement.
Well, we were introduced.
We didn't meet in the [LAUGHS] bar.
Irene.
Irene-- Irene Brown.
We have a mutual cousin.
He's my cousin, she's her cousin.
Well, I was-- I loved liturgical music from way, way back.
My father and my grandfather they were both very well known, in the-- in that-- not as professionals, as just plain lay men.
But they knew how to perform a beautiful davening, what we call.
On top of that, thank-- thank God, I have a fine voice.
I studied a little bit.
Not much.
But I studied with [NON-ENGLISH] University for a while.
And I am known in the-- as one of the fine interpreters in-- in davening.
I was davening in large places, very large shuls.
And mainly because I like it.
And I have a style which is today not-- [LAUGHS] there aren't too many who still have the old European style of davening.
Over here, people like easier, lighter stuff.
First of all, it's in-- it's in your blood.
Secondly, maybe it has to do with your-- in the surrounding you grew up.
I didn't-- I didn't pick up the happy-go-lucky style in davening, or rock-and-roll type of singing.
And on top of all this, my sons, as in many cases, are more religious than their parents.
And they are-- and-- this has nothing to do with my story, but just why I became-- I-- I-- why I became a cantor.
I served in nice, beautiful big communities, around New York.
A few times I was out in Montreal, too.
I was on the West Coast.
And now, the last few years, I am connected with a big, beautiful shul, but only for the high holidays-- all year around.
It's a fact that if you want to stay in the city, it's not easy.
Because the better paid positions are out of town.
Unless you want to go to the modern shuls where I wouldn't fit in, or vice versa.
Ah, yeah.
Well, both of these things I liked since childhood.
I knew always how to draw.
And I-- first-- first, I was drawing Hebrew letters.
I wrote about 800 ketubah-- which is marriage certificates, calligraphy.
I wrote about 800.
I started it way back in Europe, because they did not have anything.

Young couples got married right after the war-- this is also a fact of history-- that after the war, those lost boys and girls and-- and husbands without-- without families, surviving men who lost their wives and children, they got married, one after the other.
So, in the town where I was, there was no printed ketubah, or a marriage certificate.
So my dean said, Chaim, take a pen.
You know how to write-- and write.
So I started to write.
Today, I have here a beautiful piece of sample.
If you wanted me, I can put it on here.
I have it inside-- one of the ketubahs.
Then I went into designing for embroidery.
This might be a little bit surprising to all of you.
The United States landed on the moon in 1957-- no, no, no-- '68.
And Apollo, you remember Apollo?
Apollo 11-- and I made the design for the Apollo.
And a big company, an embroidery company, who was working for NASA, they had the contract.
But I was the head designer for the-- for the Apollo emblems, the patches which their astronauts wore on their uniforms.
Apollo 8, 9, 10, 11. 11 went up. 11 landed on the moon. 12, I did. 13 I didn't do anymore.
And then 13-- Apollo 13 had an accident-- not because I didn't do the patch, but it's so it did happen.
I still have the original patch.
I still have the patch.
It's in my room.
I hope I have it in my room.
So, this-- I became a designer in the embroidery field.

The company went broke, and I-- and I got a little bit older, thank [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I'm still active, I still draw, I still sing, and I still try to do-- I wanted to ask you, is there any message-- well, I think we're going to pause now.
OK We're going to go--
